questions.question: What is This presentation uses a free template provided by FPPT.com www.free -power -point -templates.comCSC 309: Artificial Intelligence Ali Ahmad Aminu , PhD Computer Science Department, Gombe State University 2025/2026 Session This presentation uses a free template provided by FPPT.com www.free -power -point -templates.comWeek 4 : Rule Based System and Expert System This presentation uses a free template provided by FPPT.com www.free -power -point -templates.comWeek 4: Outline •Rules for knowledge representation •Rule Based (Production) System •Definition •Main components •Rule Execution Cycle •Conflict resolution •Applications •Expert System This presentation uses a free template provided by FPPT.com www.free -power -point -templates.comRules for Knowledge Representation •Rules?
questions.answer: used as one way to represent knowledge

questions.question: What is •Rules?
questions.answer: expressed in the following form: If <conditions> then <conclusion> Example: If temperature is below 10 then weather is cold 4Then -pattern, Conclusion or ConsequentIf-pattern, Premise Or Antecedent This presentation uses a free template provided by FPPT.com www.free -power -point -templates.comRules for Knowledge Representation •Rules are usually expressed in the form of IF

questions.question: What is statements, such as: • IF A THEN B •A?
questions.answer: called the antecedent and B is the consequent in this statement This can be considered to have a similar logical meaning as the following: • A → B •In expressing rules, the consequent usually takes the form of an action or a conclusion

questions.question: What is This presentation uses a free template provided by FPPT.com www.free -power -point -templates.comRules for Knowledge Representation •Generally, a rule can have: •more than one antecedent, usually combined either by AND or by OR connective •more than one consequent, which usually suggests that there?
What is •For example, suitable antecedents in a rule might be: IF x > 3 IF name?
questions.answer: “Bob” IF weather is cold • Here, the objects being considered are x, name, and weather; the operators are “>” and “is”, and the values are 3, “Bob,” and cold
